fact: March 4 is exactly 63 days into the year - that's 9 weeks perfectly!
In the Northern Hemisphere, March 4 sits in the middle of spring's arrival, when tree sap typically starts flowing like nature's own plumbing system.
March 4 often falls right in the peak of maple syrup season in North America - trees are literally bleeding sugar water!
On this date, the sun usually rises about 6 minutes earlier than it did just one week ago - daylight is racing back!
March 4 lands in Pisces territory on the zodiac calendar, making it a 'water sign' day.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, March 4 typically marks the beginning of autumn's crispy leaf season.
Earthworms often start wiggling closer to the surface around March 4 in northern climates, getting ready for spring's big soil party.
March 4 sits exactly 17 days before the spring equinox - close enough that you can almost smell the flowers coming!
In northern regions, March 4 is often prime time for hearing the first 'crack' sounds of ice breaking up on lakes and rivers.
March 4 typically falls during the time when many animals are shedding their thick winter coats, leaving fuzzy tumbleweeds of fur everywhere.
On March 4, the moon can appear extra bright against the crisp late-winter air, since there's usually less humidity to blur its glow.
This date often marks peak time for finding the weirdest icicle shapes in cold climates, right before they all start melting into puddles.
